summary: DeVault argues that software can and should be considered 'completed,' pushing back against the common assumption that inactive repositories are abandoned or inferior. He distinguishes between internal and external sources of change, noting that all change—even bug fixes—introduces instability. Projects should draw a finish line or gradually raise the bar for new features. He cites Sway and scdoc as examples of projects that have met their goals and now focus solely on stability and reliability.
key_points: The software community wrongly equates low commit activity with abandonment, when it often signals maturity
All change—new features, dependency updates, even bug fixes—introduces instability, so managing change is essential for reliability
Projects should define a finish line or progressively raise the threshold for accepting new features
Sway and scdoc are presented as models of completed software that now focus only on stability
Maintainers have a responsibility to critically evaluate feature requests in terms of their cost to complexity and stability
money_quotes: There's an idea which encounters a bizarre level of resistance from the broader software community: that software can be completed.
A git repository which doesn't receive many commits is abandoned or less worthwhile. For my part, I consider software that aims to be completed to be more worthwhile most of the time.
Any maintained program will naturally increase in stability over time as bug fixes accumulate, towards some finite maximum. However, change drives this trend in reverse.
If you want to produce software which is reliable, robust, and stable, then managing change is an essential requirement.
Software projects can, and often should, draw a finish line.
Becoming a tool which 'just works' and can be depended on without a second thought is the only goal going forward.
A slow trickle of commits is the ultimate fate of software which aims to be stable and reliable.
Turning a critical eye to new feature requests, and evaluating their cost in terms of complexity and stability, is another responsibility that your users are depending on you for.
key_insight: Software that aims to be completed and resists unnecessary change delivers more stability and reliability than software that chases perpetual feature growth.
